speaker: interviewee
text: It must have been in April, I suppose, because where she was-- [AUDIO OUT]

speaker: interviewer
text: We have speed. OK, Jeanne.

speaker: interviewer
text: Today is August 15, 1995. We're interviewing Catheryne Morgan. This is-- I'm Jeanne Muchnick, the interviewer. We're in New York, New York. And the interview will be in English.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hi, my name is Jeanne Muchnick, M-U-C-H-N-I-C-K. Today is August 15, 1995. And we're interviewing survivor Catheryn Morgan, New York, New York. Mrs. Morgan, will you introduce yourself to us? Tell us your name, your maiden name, and when you were born and where you were born.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. I'm Catheryn Morgan, nee Catarina Ilkovic, born in Vrutky, Czechoslovakia, April 21, '28.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell-- first spell your maiden name, and then spell the name of the town.

speaker: interviewee
text: I-L-K-- I'm sorry.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I-L-K-O-V-I-C.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the name of the town in Czechoslovakia?

speaker: interviewee
text: V-R-U-T-K-Y.

speaker: interviewer
text: Say it one more time.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's in northern Slovakia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Say it one more time, because I think I was talking.

speaker: interviewee
text: V-R-U-T-K-Y. Vrutky.

speaker: interviewer
text: In northern Czechoslovakia.

speaker: interviewee
text: Northern Czechoslovakia, right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your father's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's name was Eugene. My mother's name was Elizabeth. And I had one sister called Eva, who was three years older.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your mother's maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mother's maiden name was Klein, and she had one sister, Rose.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you grow up in a house or an apartment?

speaker: interviewee
text: I grew up in an apartment in Vrutky. My father had a pharmacy, and we lived in the same building where the pharmacy was. But my grandparents had a beautiful villa in Vrutky where we spent most of our time, since they had a huge garden. And so my sister and I and, as a matter of fact, my aunt Rose's son George, we spent all summers right there. And I-- we stayed there until about 1932, to the best of my recollection, when we moved to Ruzonberg. It's another town in the same area. And we left Vrutky.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell the name of that town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ruzonberg would be R-U-Z-O-N-B-E-R-G.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did your mother work?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother worked with my father in the pharmacy, as a matter of fact, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the grandparents, they were your mother's parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's parents, and they were what you would call upper-middle class. They were quite well-to-do. My grandfather had three stone quarries. I remember the name of two of them, [NON-ENGLISH] and [NON-ENGLISH] And the third one, I don't know. And they were quite well-to-do. And on my mother's side, we were related to Gustav and Rosa Lang, who was the sister of Mr. Hertz, from the famous Hertz [NON-ENGLISH] family. And we were the last one to see them before they were-- when they were taken away, in fact.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you have memories of your grandparents, or the kind of people they were?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, very vivid and very beautiful memories, as I said. In the beautiful villa, with the glorious garden-- they had a Dresden gardener, and to his dismay, we children were always in the flowers. And they were truly lovely, lovely memories. That was preschool for me. And then we moved to-- when I moved to [NON-ENGLISH], I started school there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about your father's parents? Did you know them?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's parents, I knew only my grandfather. I never met the grandmother. He visited once. He was a very tall, lean gentleman. And to the best of my recollection, he must have died while I was still in-- we were still in Vrutky. But I've never visited the-- the place where they lived, and I really wouldn't even know, actually, where.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many years of school did you go to? Can you tell us?

speaker: interviewee
text: All told? Well, I started school in [NON-ENGLISH]. I went to elementary school. And we lived there until 1939. From '33 through '39, which would be six years. And then we moved to a place when things were getting bad. That was Czechoslovakia. We fled to Hungary. And we fled to an area which was once Hungary, and once Czechoslovakia. It changed hands a couple of times. The place is called [NON-ENGLISH], and it was near Uzhgorod.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell that again?

speaker: interviewee
text: Uzhgorod would be-- well, being that it was Hungary, it would be Ungvar, which would be U-N-G-V-A-R. And by then I was in high school. And I went to the Hebrew high school, where under relatively normal condition, we really went to school up until March, 1944, when the German marched into Hungary.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's go back a little to school. What do-- do you remember what a typical day was in elementary school? Can you go back that far?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, not really for a typical day, but it was very normal. And as you know, Czechoslovakia was a very democratic country. It was patterned after the USA, and conditions were very nice. I never knew anything about anti-Semitism. I was a excellent student. And school years were happy ones, and without any incidents that I should, really, remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was-- what was your family like, in terms of religion? Were they active in the Jewish community?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we were not. Not at all. I knew that we were Jewish, of course. And my mother would light candle Friday evenings. But otherwise, we really did not keep a what you call an orthodox home. Still, of course, I was well-aware that we were Jewish. And we went to Hebrew classes in school, where we were taught Bible and some [INAUDIBLE]. But that-- that was about the extent of my Jewish background.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you were growing up, what languages do you speak at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- that was very important to us. We were very chauvinistic, and we only spoke Czech, while my parents-- I caught them many times speaking either Hungarian or German, because when they were born, that was belonged to Austro-Hungaria. But we children, we would never to be caught talking Hungarian. In fact, I didn't know any until we fled to Hungary. And from one day to the next, I was sitting in school, and it was totally foreign to me. And I had to learn it in a big hurry in order to continue school.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were growing up as a young girl, were you and your sister very close?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we were very close. She was three years older, but we were extremely close, and we did a lot of things together. And of course, I looked up to her as a bigger sister. And she was beautiful and lady like. I was a real tomboy and terrible and she was really my example how to be a lady.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you share a room growing up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we had a beautiful room, just for the two of us. As I said, we were quite well-to-do, so we lived under very nice and comfortable conditions, and we had our own room. I even remember the color. It was willow green. And we had our own room.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was an apartment, right?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was an apartment. Never lived in a house.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe what the apartment was like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I do very well. It was a beautiful corner apartment, with an entrance hall. Then there was what they called the salon, which would be today's living room. Then from my parent's bedroom, our bedroom, kitchen, bath, and a foyer.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the exact address?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember the address. I remember the address of my school. But funny, I don't remember that address, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the address of the school?

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you walk to school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Ruzonberg was small, but it was a town. It was not a village. And it was a town, and we walked to school. Absolutely.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were-- were there other Jewish families?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, there were. Yes. Indeed, several of my friends were, and some were not. And there was a synagogue. And there was, unfortunately, I really don't know. [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you say it was 20% Jewish, or less, or more, the town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Certainly not more. Probably the most would be 20%, I would assume.

speaker: interviewer
text: So some of your friends were not Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Were not Jewish, and we were very close friends. I know that for Passover, we had the matzah, and that had to be brought in, I remember vividly, from somewhere else, because there were none available in Ruzonberg itself. So it must have been a small Jewish community. And the ones that were, we were rather assimilated, as the cliche goes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. Were girls allowed to date non-Jewish boys growing up?

speaker: interviewee
text: I wasn't in that stage of dating, so I wouldn't know anything about that. Of course, I was much too young.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you get to see your grandparents often, after they had--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we remained a very close-knit family, since my grandparents had only two children-- my mother and Aunt Rose. And we were very close, indeed. And every Sunday, my grandfather with his chauffeur and a beautiful [? hotmobile ?] car would pick us up, and we would go in the nearby countryside or to a nearby resort. There we would have all dinner on a lovely terrace. I have recollections of that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what your hobbies were like, as a young girl?

speaker: interviewee
text: Me?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. Did you-- what you did after school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sports. I skied before I walked, and rode a two-wheel bicycle before I walked, and did all the things that little boys do instead of little girls.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you say you didn't feel any anti-Semitism at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: None at all, no. None whatsoever. I was never aware that I'm any different than the other kids, except for religion. We went-- as I said, we attended some-- whatever you call it. Bible classes, or whatever.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any dreams when you were a young girl of what you wanted to be when you grew up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, well, my father always wanted us to follow in his footsteps and study either chemistry or be a pharmacist. So we sort of had dreamt of that. But I was much too young to make any decision to that, since that was way before you would have to decide.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, do you remember what the first signs of danger were, when you fled to Hungary?

speaker: interviewee
text: Only that it was 1939, and the [NON-ENGLISH] was occupied by Germans. And we heard rumors. And we also heard rumor that some men were taken away to the police station, and they were beaten, but I have never seen it. And my father-- we always felt very secure, because my father was a high-ranking officer, once upon a time. And-- and in fact, he had the only pharmacy. So it was called like a vital occupation. So somehow, it-- we were not affected by it.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were still quite young, but do you remember your parents talking about the war or any--

speaker: interviewee
text: They did, to a certain extent. And my father, bless his memory, he just refused to believe that it can happen. They, evidently, heard the rumors. And in fact, he had many connections in Switzerland, because he was a pharmacist, and through the drug companies. And they urged him to come, and to flee.

speaker: interviewer
text: But of course, who would leave under normal condition your everything, and your families, and be uprooted? And he just never believed that it can happen, in spite of the fact that, towards the end, that I remember hearing rumors-- that people were actually taken away from their homes.

speaker: interviewee
text: He had an older brother called Sigmund, who was also a pharmacist. And they had a pharmacy in Nitra. And they remained in Czechoslovakia. And we heard that they were taken away. And we heard some indirect rumors that the situation was quite, quite serious and bad in that area. But that's the last we heard from them, never again. They had one daughter, Vera. I understand she survived the war. I tried to locate her, but I never could.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any conversations with your sister, wondering what might happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: About war years?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really, except that towards the end, she told me she had a dreadful dream that we were all being herded-- I mean, there was no name of concentration camps-- like to one area, and that we all died. I'll never forget it. She told me that, yes. But we were not aware of any immediate danger, so to speak. But I do remember, vividly, as I said, that particular--

speaker: interviewer
text: When did your life start to really change?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it changed drastically and rapidly on that fateful day, when the German marched into Hungary, which was, I believe, March the 17th. I'm not sure. I know it's in March.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was that year?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was 1944. And like from one day to the next, school stopped. Life stopped, as such. And suddenly, we find ourself-- my father was-- and us, we were just asked to leave the apartment and the pharmacy. And they moved us. First we lived in, I don't know, somebody else's apartment for a few days. And a few days later, we were all taken to Uzhgorod, to Ungvar, where-- it wasn't a ghetto, but it was a gathering area in an old brick factory, which was, like, in the open. And that's where, obviously, they brought in everyone from the neighboring villages and towns. And that's where we stayed-- and mercifully, I don't remember how long-- under very primitive condition, until we were taken, then, by train on that fateful day to Auschwitz. How long we stayed there, I have no recollection.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember on that day packing up anything out of your apartment? Any personal possessions?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, none. As a child, I don't remember. I only remember-- vaguely, I remember that my father told my mother to hide her engaged-- her-- obviously, her wedding band and her engagement ring. But no personal possession that I recall at all, that I had taken along.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember, were you in school that day, or were you home?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, this was after school that we found out, and we never returned to school again. It was abrupt, sudden, and without, so to speak, really any prior warning.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then when you were at this gathering place at the brick factory, did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: This was where the Hungarians had taken us. And I will never forgive them, because they were worse than the Germans. They did it on their own, over-zealously. And they herded us all in. My grandmother, who-- I forgot to say. In the meantime, my grandfather passed away. And my grandmother lived with us. So it was my grandmother, my Aunt Rose, my mother and father, and my sister. And we were all in this little cubicle, which wasn't much privacy, because the whole thing was an open area. Still, we stuck together. And the only thing I remember is that one day, my grandmother complained-- may she rest in peace-- that she was hungry. And we couldn't give her anything, because there was none.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about your Aunt Rose's son?

speaker: interviewee
text: Aunt Rose's son fled. They were divorced, and his family lived in Rome. And they took him to Rome. And he spent his whole war years in Italy, in Rome. And Aunt Rose stayed with us. She lived with us. And she's very special, if I may say, now. She's very special. And I'm glad that I have this opportunity to mention, because while my mother gave me life the first time, Aunt Rose, truly the second. Because I was with her in Auschwitz and afterwards. And if not for her, I surely would have perished later. I was emaciated and ill. And she brought me, literally, back to life.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was-- what was her son's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: George. He's still alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did they-- her-- her ex-husband, they had some inkling? How did they know to send him to Italy?

speaker: interviewee
text: They, obviously, must have heard that things are bad. And he, himself, lived in France. And he took the son to Italy, to his sisters, who were married to Italians that lived in Rome.

speaker: interviewer
text: So-- and you don't remember exactly how long you were in this brick factory that all of you--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I have no recollection. My-- it was days, maybe weeks, but certainly not months, because by May we were in Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they didn't really give you any food?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not that I recall. None.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Evidently, whatever we had with us. And eventually, that dwindled down.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what you slept on? Did you sleep on the floor?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, mercifully, I don't.

speaker: interviewer
text: So describe what it was like in May, when you were sent away to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewee
text: The only thing I remember, being taken to the train. And we were searched. And it took hours and hours. And again, the overseer was Hungarian policeman with that unforgettable feather cap they had. They were herding us, and they were yelling and screaming. And they said, if you don't give us what you have now, we will shoot you. And that I remember vividly. And then, before we were herded, one terrible memory that will always remain with me, they ask my sister and me-- leaving my parents, and they took us to the building. At the time, we didn't know why. And we were so naive. We were so young. And there was a whole group of them, and they made us undress-- totally naked. They never harmed us. They never touched us. But they made us undress. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And then after a while, they send us back to my parents. And I heard them laugh, of course, and making remarks. Obviously, what, mercifully I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So at-- at this point, you were still all together.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were all together. We were taken in to this dreadful train, and we were herded into the train, on the way to Auschwitz. How many days it took, I have no recollection. Conditions were dreadful. I remember very little of it. And all I know is that hum of the train-- ever since then, I detest trains, and I can't look at them. In fact, when we're out in a station, and there is a-- a-- what do they call the train that are-- not the passenger, the other kind.

speaker: interviewer
text: The freight trains.

speaker: interviewee
text: The freight trains. My husband always tells me, look away.

speaker: interviewer
text: But when you were herded in the trains, you didn't know where you were going.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we did not. We did not where we were herded. The whole family, lots of other people into the cattle car, and off we went. How many days, I don't know. And we don't-- we had no idea where we are heading, what we ate, or how we-- mercifully, I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your grandmother was with you at this time.

speaker: interviewee
text: The grandmother, my aunt, mother and father, and sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old was-- were your parents? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. My mother was barely 42 years old, 43.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were about how-- you were about--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember there being food at all on the train?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't remember eating anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember being very hot or very cold?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, neither. I guess when you are a kid, you don't-- you are not aware of that. And as far as food, mercifully, it's blocked.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so what happened when the train doors opened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Finally, the train's door opened. And the first thing we heard, yelling and screaming of inmates who came to the train in striped suits. We didn't know who they were, but all-- we heard them yelling in all kind of languages, they-- give the children to the old. Give the children to the old. Well, being that we didn't have small children, we were one group. The next thing we know, my father was gone. We never had a chance to say goodbye. He disappeared, and the next thing we know is, we were standing in front of the infamous Dr. Mengele, and my grandmother to the left, my sister, my mother, and myself and Aunt Rose to the right. And that's the last we saw my grandparents. And we were herded, then, into some barracks, where for hours and hours, we were standing. And obviously, by then I remember being thirsty. There was no water. And were taken to-- and they told us we are being taken to a shower. But we never saw a shower, but they started to shave off our hair. And we were-- they took our clothes, incidentally, and we were thrown some dreadful rags. And we looked at it-- we couldn't see ourselves. We had no mirror. But we could see each other. And it was a dreadful sight, with the shaven heads. And then, God knows how many hours, or maybe, a day later, finally, we were taken to the barracks in Auschwitz, in Lager B, where we were still together, the four of us, my mother, Aunt Rose, my sister, and myself. And that's where life started. They didn't need us to work. We were not [INAUDIBLE] anymore. That was towards the end. We came in with a transport of gypsies, incidentally. I understand the poor gypsies were taken immediately to the gas chambers. And we did not work. But we had these terrible zahlappells, which are infamous by now. People seem to realize just what it was, twice a day. And we felt so bad for our mother, standing. And I have two things I remember about Auchwitz-- the blazing sun, and the mud. It was always gray, and muddy, and cold. And we had no clothes. And for hours on end, we were standing for the count. But we were never beaten, and we were never sent to work.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was a typical day like? What time did they call you in the morning?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it must have been very early, because it was still dark. And they herded us out. I guess that's when you had the facilities for the infamous toilets, which, luckily, I don't remember much of. And then there were-- all I remember, standing outside in rows of five, to the best of my recollection. And they counted, and counted, and recounted us. And whoever couldn't stand, of course, passed out. And there was no end to it. And then we were herded back. And then, I guess, came the meal, which consisted of some kind of a black liquid with bread that the girls put certain-- girls were-- I don't know that they were volunteers, or they were drafted to bring it from the kitchen. And we were given that. And we just stayed, until the next zahlappell, which wasn't too long after. And that went on, day in, day out.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how-- how did you sleep? On what--

speaker: interviewee
text: We slept in-- on these wooden bunks. There were about five of us to each row. And we tried to keep neat and clean and wash ourselves as much as we could, of course. But it was getting increasingly difficult. We started to lose weight, rapidly. And things got from bad to worse. And we were looking over with envy. It was mostly German women who came out there, and they were doing the count. And they came with their beautiful, freshly-ironed blouses. And they wore boots. And to this day, I hate culottes, they all wore. And I-- and you know, what you do for horses and all that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, the whips?

speaker: interviewee
text: The whip, in her hand.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember, at this point, any conversations with your mother or sister or grandmother?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. Grandmother was gone. We didn't have her with.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, right. Your grandma, right. Your Aunt Rose, I meant.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we just-- I don't remember, except that we-- she must have felt terrible, for to see her children. And we felt, in turn, terrible for her being out there. And each day's selection came while we were on the zahlappell. Each day was a selection, and each day we were grateful that we are still together. And that went on until the day that will live in infamy, October 10, 1944, when Mengele came to the barracks, himself. And that was the final selection. And while my aunt and I went to the right, my sister and mother to the left. And that's the last time that I have seen them. We were taken from there to a railroad station. We didn't know where we go. It was dark. And they told us again, we're going to some showers. So obviously, we must have gone through some kind of a disinfecting station. And the next thing I knew, I was again on a train. And they took us to a place that I cannot find on the map. It was called [NON-ENGLISH]. We were the so-called able-bodied, my aunt and I. And there was a relatively small camp, women only. And we were taken for slave labor. We were digging ditches, anti-tank ditches. And they took us out every morning in this bitter cold, with a little rag on our bones. And we had to-- I had a shovel that I could hardly lift. And we were, allegedly, doing this work from morning until night, outdoors. We were back to the barracks, and we stayed there until about, almost January or end of December of 1944.

speaker: interviewer
text: [NON-ENGLISH], you think it was--

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: You think it was in Poland, near Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: We keep thinking, because it couldn't be too far from Auschwitz. Only it was a very short train ride, and I cannot find it on the map. But it was definitely a slave labor camp for women only. And as I said, there were the anti-tank ditches. By then, of course, rumors were-- and even we heard it-- that the Russians are approaching. So one day, they herded us into a row. And that's when the infamous death march to Bergen-Belsen started.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's just go back a little bit. When you were at Auschwitz, and you were living in the barracks, did you-- did you know then what was going on there, that--

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- yes, we were-- we were aware. And we also heard-- I mean, there were some prisoners that came around doing some odd duties. I don't know who they were. They were political prisoner, men. And they were telling us that it's [GERMAN], which means, of course, that it's a camp where we are for one purpose only. And that sooner or later, you see that smoke, they would point out. That's where you're going to wind up yourself. And they are the ones who told us that the children were thrown alive into these pits. And older people were taken to the-- what they called, euphemistically, a shower, which was, of course, the gas chambers. So we found out from them, otherwise we wouldn't have known. We-- and the smell, of course. Then we realized the dreadful smell, and we knew those were the burned bodies that we smelled.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you think, or did you know then that-- about your grandmother and your father's fate?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we did-- we had no knowledge where. We tried to think that they weren't taken, indeed, right away to the gas chambers.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this whole time, you really still harbored hope that they were alive.

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course, absolutely. Even afterwards. After the war, I thought I'd find my family. I thought they would manage to escape.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your sister and your mother, were they sickly? Is that why they were--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not at all-- except, of course, we were thin. But we all were. My mother was relatively young. The mistake was, they were told us then, that automatically he sent one to the left, one to the right. And we went in succession. We should have not. Perhaps we would have been together. But unfortunately, he just sort of automatically pushed right and left, and--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there ever any goodbye, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: None. None whatsoever. The following day was my sister's birthday, October the 11th. And I remember that we all saved some bread, so we can [CRIES] give it to her. But it never came to it. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And that was the last time that I was in Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll stop, and we'll change tapes here.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.
